serving.description: 1 serving (approx. 120 g cooked lean lamb leg)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled lean lamb leg is approximately 120 g of cooked lean meat, equivalent to a palm-sized portion without bone.

All per_100g core values are taken from this source without modification. Fatty acid fractions (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082 as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Negligible carbohydrate food; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled lean lamb leg contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to clarify that no true GI measurement exists. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (65 mg/100 g) with favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (potassium 310 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Grilled lean lamb leg is suitable for individuals managing hypertension when prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces, as its naturally low sodium content and meaningful potassium level support blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (30.6 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled lean lamb leg produces no glycaemic response due to its complete absence of carbohydrates and sugar, making it a suitable high-protein food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with non-starchy vegetables and consumed without sugary glazes or sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.5 g/100 g), cholesterol (80 mg/100 g), and trace ruminant trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) present despite lean trimming
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled lean lamb leg is acceptable in moderate portions for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its lean profile and high protein content, but saturated fat and cholesterol levels warrant limiting portion frequency to 2–3 times per week and avoiding added fat during cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat drives pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; trace ruminant trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled lean lamb leg is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its red meat arachidonic acid content and trace ruminant trans fat, both of which promote inflammatory pathways relevant to RA; substitution with oily fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Lamb Leg, Lean is a cooked red meat product derived from the hind leg of a young sheep (lamb), with all separable visible fat removed before or after grilling. The meat is grilled until fully cooked through, developing a slightly charred exterior while retaining a tender, juicy interior with a characteristic mild gamey flavour typical of lamb. As a lean cut, it is notably high in protein at 30.6 g per 100 g and relatively low in fat at 6.0 g per 100 g, with an energy density of 177 kcal per 100 g. It contains zero carbohydrates and zero sugar, making it entirely non-glycaemic and suitable for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 65 mg per 100 g, and potassium is 310 mg per 100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines for blood pressure management. Iron content is 2.6 mg per 100 g, contributing meaningfully to daily iron requirements. Cholesterol is 80 mg per 100 g and saturated fat is approximately 2.5 g per 100 g, which are moderate values for a lean red meat. A trace amount of ruminant conjugated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is naturally present. This food is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a main protein component in meals across Middle Eastern, South Asian, and international cuisines, and is particularly relevant in Singapore's mamak and halal food contexts. Clinically, it is suitable for diabetes and hypertension management but should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease and avoided by those with rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory red meat profile.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in grilled lamb?
Is lamb leg suitable for diabetics?
Is grilled lamb leg low in sodium?
Can I eat lamb leg on a keto diet?
How many calories in grilled lean lamb leg?
Is lamb leg good for high blood pressure?
What is the fat content of lean grilled lamb?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06050009, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Source match is exact for this food item. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lamb leg lean cooked (NDB 17082) to supplement the HPB record, which does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this supplementation is documented in notes.
notes: 1. ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — lamb leg lean is a pure animal muscle protein with no carbohydrate content. sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal product containing no plant fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17082). 2. FATTY ACID SUPPLEMENTATION: saturated_fat_g (2.5), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.3), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4), and trans_fat_g (0.1) are not separately reported in the HPB source for this record. These values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17082 — Lamb, leg, whole (shank and sirloin), separable lean only, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, cooked, roasted — as the closest approved-source equivalent for lean cooked lamb leg fatty acid profile. The grilling method versus roasting is not expected to materially alter fatty acid fractions. This supplementation is documented as an adapted source match. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this specific entry (F06050009). While USDA SR Legacy does report some of these for lamb leg, the HPB record is the primary source and supplementation of all micronutrients from a secondary source would exceed the adapted-source policy for this record. Follow-up enrichment from USDA FoodData Central is recommended. 4. GI: glycemic_index is set to the numeric value 0 as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to clarify that no true GI measurement exists for this negligible-carbohydrate food (0 g/100 g). glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. This food is a pure protein/fat product; GI is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact code exists for grilled lean lamb leg specifically. No SNOMED CT code assigned to avoid fabrication. 6. MULTILINGUAL: This food is classified as Generic/International and does not fall under a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine category requiring mandatory local-language names. The input record assigns primary_cuisine as mamak, which is a Singaporean-Indian Muslim cuisine context; however, grilled lean lamb leg as a generic food item does not have established canonical mamak-specific local-language names distinct from the English name. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi names are assigned to avoid fabrication of unverified translations. This is documented as a gap for follow-up review.
